name: dez

metadata.name: dez
classifiers: Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules
metadata.summary: A set of pyevent-based network services

documentation: The dez library includes an asynchronous server benchmarking toolset; advanced, inotify-based static caching; XML and JSON stream parsing; Stomp, OP, HTTP, and WebSocket servers; and WebSocketProxy, which enables web application deployment on existing, unmodified TCP servers without straying from the HTML5 spec. In addition, dez offers a highly sophisticated API for implementing custom protocols, as well as a controller class that simplifies the creation of applications that require multiple servers to run in the same process.

classes.name: dez.buffer.Buffer

methods.name: dez.buffer.Buffer.get_value
documentation:
Return the data in consume mode, or the remainder of the data in
index mode.

methods.name: dez.buffer.Buffer.get_full_value
documentation: Returns the full value of the string, even in index mode.

methods.name: dez.buffer.Buffer.exhaust
documentation:
In index mode, sets position to the end of the buffered data.  In
consume mode, sets data to empty.

methods.name: dez.buffer.Buffer.reset_position
documentation: Resets position to 0.

methods.name: dez.buffer.Buffer.reset
documentation:
Resets data to empty, or an opional 'content' string, and position
to 0.

methods.name: dez.buffer.Buffer.empty
documentation: Boolean; is true if the the buffer is empty

methods.name: dez.buffer.Buffer.__add__
documentation: Add the passed-in string to the buffer

A Buffer object buffers text, and has two modes, 'index' and 'consume'

In index mode, the buffer object keeps track of a position 'pos',
which can be altered by calling b.move, or by b.exhaust, which
moves 'pos' to the end of the file.

In consume mode (default), a buffer is essentially just a proxy
for its contained string b.data.

If you experience memory leaks, make sure you don't have
buffers sitting around in 'index' mode.

classes.name: dez.buffer.ReadBuffer
documentation:
This works exactly like the Buffer class, except it
decode()s incoming bytes before passing them off

classes.name: dez.buffer.WriteBuffer
documentation:
This works exactly like the Buffer class, with the
addition of send()

classes.name: dez.buffer.B64ReadBuffer

methods.name: dez.buffer.B64ReadBuffer.__add__
documentation: Add the passed-in base64-encoded string to the pre-buffer

This works exactly like the ReadBuffer class, except it

methods.name: dez.buffer.B64WriteBuffer.get_value

This works exactly like the WriteBuffer class, except
get_value() returns a base64-encoded string

def connect(self,hostname,port,cb=None):
s = io.client_socket(hostname,port)
conn = Connection((hostname,port), s)
#TODO pass the callback on to Connection, don't cal it here
cb(conn)

functions.name: dez.http.application.serve_wsgi_application

Serve a WSGI application using a paste.deploy.config

This is an adapter to allow dez to be used with the paster
serve mechanism as interpreted through a *.ini file.

classes.name: dez.http.application.HTTPApplication

methods.name: dez.http.application.HTTPApplication.__init__
documentation:
start listening on the given port (this doesn't include a call to
rel.dispatch)

methods.name: dez.http.application.HTTPApplication.start
documentation: calls rel.dispatch

methods.name: dez.http.application.HTTPApplication.add_proxy_rule
documentation:
Adds a prefix that will be proxied to the destination
http_app.add_proxy_rule("/chat/", "127.0.0.1", 4700)
url == "/chat/index.html"  # will be proxied
url == "/hello/chat/index.html" # will not be proxied

methods.name: dez.http.application.HTTPApplication.add_static_rule
documentation:
Adds a prefix that will be served as a static directory or file
http_app.add_static_rule("/static/", "/home/app/static")
url == "/static/css/common.css" refers to /home/app/static/css/common.css

http_app.add_static_rule("/", "/home/app/static/html/index.html")
url == "/" refers to /home/app/static/html/index.html

http_app.add_static_rule("/main.html", "/home/app/static/html/main.html")
url == "/main.html" refers to /home/app/static/html/main.html
url == "/main.html/foo" returns a 404

methods.name: dez.http.application.HTTPApplication.add_cb_rule
documentation:
Adds a prefix that will issue a call to cb when a request with that
            url prefix is matched. This works the same as the HTTPDaemon's
            register_prefix function.

            def dispatch(req):
                req.write("HTTP/1.0 200 OK

Hello World")
                req.close()

            http_app.add_cb_rule("/helloworld", dispatch)

methods.name: dez.http.application.HTTPApplication.add_wsgi_rule
documentation:
Adds a prefix that will execute (call) a wsgi compliant app function.
Will provide the app with the appropriate environ and start_response
function.

HTTPApplication depends on HTTPDaemon for its prefix ordering behavior.
It issues cbs as per add_cb_rule in the same manner that HTTPDaemon
does as well -- it simply passes a dez.http.server.request.HTTPRequest
instance as the single argument to the callback. Its up to the user to
construct the appropriate RawHTTPResponse or HTTPResponse as required.

methods.name: dez.http.server.response.WSGIResponse.body_cb
documentation:
response body transmission happens here

Per PEP 333, we ensure that the headers have not already
been sent. (which is a little silly since we are sending
the entire response at once)

methods.name: dez.http.server.response.WSGIResponse.start_response
documentation:
implements start_response per PEP 333

We enforce:
    - if exception info has been sent, then ensure that
      the headers have not already been sent.
    - start_response has not already been called.

methods.name: dez.http.client.response.HTTPProtocolReader.__recv_status
documentation: def test_function(self, data):
self.test_buff += data
print 'buff: "%s"'%self.test_buff
if self.test_buff.endswith("\r\n"):
self.__recv_status(self.test_buff[:-2])
self.test_buff = ""
return
self.conn.set_rmode_size(1, self.test_function)
